# SIR on Networks (post-midterm)

Companion to **SIR and Epidemics.md** (homogeneous mixing); this note covers SIR on $G(n,p)$ and the configuration model, plus the SIR ODEs.

## Setup: Infection digraph

Each vertex draws recovery time $T_i\sim\mu$ i.i.d. Conditional on $\{T_i\}$, for each edge $ij$ independently draw it as "open" with prob $p_{T_i} = 1-e^{-\beta T_i}$.
$C_+(v)=R(\infty)=$ vertices ever-infected starting from $v$ = those reachable in the infection digraph.

---

## Theorem 1 (Bow-tie / two-stage BP for $G(n,p)$ SIR, $p=c/n$)

For homogeneous mixing on $K_{n+1}$ or $G(n,p)$:
- If $R_0 = c\mathbb E[p_T] < 1$: $R_\infty$ is bounded in probability.
- If $R_0 > 1$: $\Pr(R_\infty\geq n^{1/3}) \to P>0$, and conditioned on $R_\infty\geq n^{1/3}$, $R_\infty/n\to\theta>0$ where
$$\theta + e^{-\theta R_0} = 1,\quad\text{equivalently}\quad 1-P = \mathbb E_T[e^{-cP\,p_T}].$$

**Proof sketch (BFS + concentration).** Track $N_t$ = #susceptibles after $t$ infections, $\Delta_t$ = #ever-infected. Each newly infected node draws $X_t\sim\text{Bin}(N_{t-1}, \tfrac{c}{n}p_{T_t})$ new infections. By independence the BP approximation $X_t^+\sim\text{Bin}(n, \tfrac{c}{n}p_T)$ couples to $X_t$ with error $O(t/n)$, valid for $t\leq n^{1/3}$. Subcriticality (rate function bound, see midterm SIR note) gives $\Pr(R_\infty\geq k)\leq e^{-(1-c)^2 k/4}$. Above criticality, BFS leaves the BP regime and a deterministic LLN takes over: $\Delta_t/n\to f(t/n)$ on the rescaled time, fixed point $\theta+e^{-c\theta}=1$ (general $\mu$: replace $c$ by $R_0$).

### Rigorous proof of the supercritical fluid limit

**Lemma 7.2.** There exists $\alpha>0$ such that for $n$ large, $R_0>1$, and $0<\epsilon\leq 1/2$, with prob $\geq 1 - O(e^{-\alpha\epsilon^2 t})$:
$$f_{-\epsilon}(t/n) \leq \tilde Y_t/n \leq f_{+\epsilon}(t/n) + O(1/n),\quad f_{\pm\epsilon}(x) = (1\pm\epsilon)\big(1-e^{-(1\pm\epsilon)R_0 x}\big) - x.$$

**Setup.** $\tilde Y_0=1$, $\tilde Y_{t+1} = \tilde Y_t + X_{t+1} - 1$, with $X_t\mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}, T_t \sim \text{Bin}(N_{t-1}, p_t)$ where $p_t = (c/n) p_{T_t}$ and $p_{T_t} = 1 - e^{-\beta T_t}$. Then $|C_+(x)| = \min\{t : \tilde Y_t \leq 0\}$.

**Why this is harder than the plain giant-component argument.** Two layers of randomness: the durations $T_i$ (which determine $p_i$), and the binomial neighbor draws given $T_i$. We apply multiplicative Chernov **twice** — once on $\sum p_i$ over the $T_i$'s, once on $\Delta_t$ given the $T_i$'s.

**Proof.**

*(i) Conditional binomial structure.* By the same survival-coupling as in P2, conditional on $T_1,\ldots,T_t$:
$$\Delta_t = X_1+\cdots+X_t \sim \text{Bin}\Big(n-1,\;1-\prod_{i=1}^t(1-p_i)\Big),\qquad \tilde Y_t = \Delta_t + 1 - t.$$

*(ii) Sandwich $\prod(1-p_i)$ and concentrate $\sum p_i$.* For $n\geq 2c$ we have $p_i\leq c/n\leq 1/2$, so Taylor of $\log(1-p_i)$ gives
$$-p_i(1+c/n) \leq \log(1-p_i) \leq -p_i,$$
and exponentiating: $e^{-(1+c/n)\sum p_i} \leq \prod_{i=1}^t(1-p_i) \leq e^{-\sum p_i}$.

Now $\sum p_i = (c/n)\sum_i Y_i$ where $Y_i = 1 - e^{-\beta T_i}\in[0,1]$ are i.i.d. with mean $\bar p$. The multiplicative Chernov bound on $\sum Y_i$ gives, with prob $\geq 1 - 2e^{-\epsilon^2 \bar p t/4}$:
$$(1-\epsilon)R_0\,\tfrac{t}{n} \leq \sum_{i=1}^t p_i \leq (1+\epsilon)R_0\,\tfrac{t}{n}\quad(R_0 := c\bar p).$$
On this event:
$$e^{-(1+c/n)(1+\epsilon)R_0\,t/n} \leq \prod_{i=1}^t(1-p_i) \leq e^{-(1-\epsilon)R_0\,t/n}.\tag{7.6}$$

*(iii) Concentrate $\Delta_t$ given the $T_i$'s.* On (7.6), use $1-e^{-x}\geq x(1-e^{-1})$ for $x\in[0,1]$:
$$\mathbb E[\Delta_t\mid T_1,\ldots,T_t] \geq (n-1)\big(1 - e^{-(1-\epsilon)R_0\,t/n}\big) \geq \tfrac{1-\epsilon}{2}t \geq t/4\quad(\epsilon\leq 1/2,\;n \text{ large}).$$
Multiplicative Chernov (P1) applied to $\Delta_t \mid T_1,\ldots,T_t$ then gives, with prob $\geq 1 - 2e^{-\epsilon^2 t/16}$:
$$(1-\epsilon)\,\mathbb E[\Delta_t\mid T_1,\ldots,T_t] \leq \Delta_t \leq (1+\epsilon)\,\mathbb E[\Delta_t\mid T_1,\ldots,T_t].\tag{7.7}$$
Both events (7.6) and (7.7) hold with prob $\geq 1 - O(e^{-\alpha\epsilon^2 t})$, $\alpha = \min(\bar p/4, 1/16)$.

*(iv) Lower envelope.* Combining (7.6) and (7.7):
$$\Delta_t \geq (1-\epsilon)(n-1)\big(1 - e^{-(1-\epsilon)R_0\,t/n}\big),$$
so
$$\tilde Y_t \geq 1 - t + (1-\epsilon)(n-1)\big(1 - e^{-(1-\epsilon)R_0\,t/n}\big) \geq n\,f_{-\epsilon}(t/n).$$

*(v) Upper envelope.* On the upper side of (7.6):
$$\mathbb E[\Delta_t\mid T_1,\ldots,T_t] \leq (n-1)\big(1 - e^{-(1+\epsilon)(1+c/n)R_0\,t/n}\big) \leq n\big(1-e^{-(1+\epsilon)R_0\,t/n}\big) + O(t/n),$$
where the second step uses $e^{-(1+c/n)R_0 t/n} = e^{-R_0 t/n}\cdot e^{-O(t/n^2)}$ and $t \leq n$. Combined with (7.7):
$$\tilde Y_t \leq 1 - t + (1+\epsilon)\mathbb E[\Delta_t\mid\cdot] \leq n(1+\epsilon)\big(1-e^{-(1+\epsilon)R_0 t/n}\big) - t + O(1) = n\big(f_{+\epsilon}(t/n) + O(1/n)\big).$$

This establishes Lemma 7.2. $\square$

**Corollary 7.3 (final outbreak size).** If $R_0>1$ and $k_n\to\infty$ with $k_n/n\to 0$, then conditioned on $R_\infty\geq k_n$, $R_\infty/n\to\theta$ in probability, where $\theta+e^{-R_0\theta}=1$. Proof: repeat the giant-component argument (iv)–(viii) with $c$ replaced by $R_0$ — geometry of $f_{\pm\epsilon}$ is structurally identical.

**Method extraction (two-layer Chernov).**
- *Recognition.* A process where each step has both a "type/weight" attached (here $T_i$) and an inner random outcome (here $\text{Bin}(N_{t-1},p_i)$).
- *First move.* Condition on the types, get binomial inner structure. Then concentrate the sum-of-types separately as $[0,1]$-valued sum via P1.
- *Pitfall.* If you don't peel the layers, you'll end up with $\sum(1-e^{-\beta T_i})$ inside an exponential and lose the Chernov hook. Also: $p_i \leq c/n$ is required for the Taylor sandwich on $\log(1-p_i)$ to drop higher-order terms.
- *Threshold takeaway.* $R_0 = c\bar p$ is the natural epidemic threshold — it's the offspring mean of the BFS-equivalent branching process averaged over recovery times.

## Lemma (Forward/backward BP perspective)
For the bow-tie picture: let $\eta = \Pr(\text{forward BP dies})$, $\eta^* = \Pr(\text{backward BP dies})$. Then
$$1-P = G_+(1-P) = \mathbb E_T[e^{-cp_T P}],\qquad 1-\theta = G_-(1-\theta) = e^{-R_0\theta}.$$
Here $G_+, G_-$ are the offspring PGFs of forward/backward BPs, with $G_-$ using degree-biased distribution.

(Same fixed-point shape as homogeneous mixing; the network just changes the offspring law.)

---

## SIR ODE limit (homogeneous mixing, $G(n,c/n)$)

Let $S(t)$ = #susceptibles, $X(t)$ = #unexplored edges out of infectives, $I(t)$ = #infected.

Stochastic dynamics (Markov rates):
- At rate $\beta X$: explore one edge. With prob $S/n$, the other endpoint is susceptible → it becomes infected, gains $\text{Poi}(c)$ new edges. Either way $X\to X-1$.
- At rate $\gamma$ each, an infective recovers ($d_v\to 0$).

## Theorem 2 (LLN for SIR ODE)
As $n\to\infty$, $(S/n, X/n, I/n)\to(s,x,i)$ deterministic, with
$$\dot s = -\beta x s,\qquad \dot x = -(\beta+\gamma)x - c\dot s,\qquad \dot i = -\gamma i - \dot s.$$

(Proof = differential-equation method à la Darling-Norris; quoted.)

## Lemma (Final size formula)
$s(\infty) = s(0)\,e^{-R_0(s(0)-s(\infty))}$ where $R_0 = c\beta/(\beta+\gamma) = c\bar p$ ($\bar p = \mathbb E[p_T]$).

**Proof.** From $\dot x = -(\beta+\gamma)x - c\dot s$ integrate $0\to\infty$ with $x(0)=x(\infty)=0$:
$$0 = -(\beta+\gamma)\int_0^\infty x\,dt - c(s(\infty)-s(0))\;\Rightarrow\; \beta\int x = \tfrac{c\beta}{\beta+\gamma}(s(0)-s(\infty)).$$
And $\dot s/s = -\beta x$ gives $s(\infty) = s(0)e^{-\beta\int x}$. Combine.

---

## SIR on Configuration Model CM($d$)

Track:
- $X(t)$ = #active half-edges (incident to infectives, unmatched)
- $S_d(t)$ = #susceptible vertices of degree $d$
- $U(t)$ = #unmatched half-edges total

Rate-of-change identities (when a $\beta$-clock rings, pair an active half-edge with a uniform unmatched one):
$$\dot U = -2\beta X,\qquad \dot S_d = -\beta d\,X/U,\qquad \dot X = -(\beta+\gamma)X - \beta X^2/U + \beta\sum_d d(d-1)S_d/U.$$

## Lemma (Closed-form via $\theta$-reduction)
Define $\theta(t) = e^{-\beta\int_0^t X/U}$. Then
$$U = U(0)\theta^2 = n\bar d\,\theta^2,\qquad S_d = S_d(0)\theta^d = p_d n\,\theta^d.$$

**Proof.** $\dot U/U = -2\beta X/U$ ⇒ integrate. $\dot S_d/S_d = -\beta d\,X/U$ ⇒ integrate.

## Theorem 3 (Single ODE for $\theta$)
$$\frac{1}{\beta+\gamma}\dot\theta = -F(\theta),\qquad F(\theta) = \bar p\big(1 - G^*(\theta)\big) - (1-\theta),$$
where $G^*(\theta) = \sum_k \tfrac{(k+1)p_{k+1}}{\bar d}\theta^k$ is the PGF of the size-biased excess degree, and $\bar p = \beta/(\beta+\gamma)$.

**Proof sketch.** Use $\beta\sum_d d(d-1)S_d/U = \beta n\sum d(d-1)p_d\theta^{d-2}/(n\bar d) = \beta\,\partial_\theta G^*(\theta)$, plug into $\dot X/X$, then with $\psi:=\theta X/U$ derive a linear ODE in $\psi$ with solution $\psi=\tfrac{\beta+\gamma}{\beta}(\theta-1)-(G^*(\theta)-1)$. Combine with $\dot\theta = -\beta\psi$.

## Lemma (Final outbreak size)
Let $\theta_\infty$ = smallest root of $F(\theta)=0$ in $(0,1)$. Then
$$R_\infty/n \;\to\; 1 - G(\theta_\infty),\quad G(\theta)=\sum_k p_k\theta^k.$$

**Why this matches forward/backward BP.** $1-\theta_\infty = \bar p(1-G^*(\theta_\infty))$ is exactly the backward-BP fixed point ($\eta^* = G^*(1-\bar p(1-\eta^*))$ in disguise), and $1-G(\theta_\infty)$ is the forward survival $1-G(\eta^*$-style pullback$)$.

---

## Force of infection / "infection runs out of high-degree first"
$$\frac{1}{S_k}\dot S_k = -\frac{k}{\theta}(\beta+\gamma)F(\theta).$$
Force is **proportional to $k$** ⇒ high-degree susceptibles deplete fastest.

---

## Recognition signals
- "Outbreak threshold on $G(n,c/n)$ / CM" → forward-BP test $R_0 > 1$.
- "Final outbreak size" → fixed-point in $\theta$ or $\eta$.
- "Why hubs get hit first" → force ∝ degree.

## Pitfalls
- For CM, the **excess** degree $G^*$ enters offspring (forward BP), not the raw $G$.
- $R_0 = c\mathbb E[p_T] = c\bar p$; missing the $\bar p$ factor (averaging over recovery time) is the most common slip.
- $\theta$ is **not** an extinction probability — it is the survival of an outgoing half-edge.

---
